data.title: Icahn Enterprises: Paying 66% Premium To Tangible NAV? For What, Exactly?
data.description:
Icahn Enterprises (NASDAQ:IEP) is Carl Icahn's flagship holding company. It's a conglomerate with stakes across energy, auto, food packaging, and public equity. For decades, Icahn built his reputation buying beaten-down companies and forcing management to unlock value in a rather aggressive way more often than not. But despite the brand power of "Icahn" on the door, I think IEP's stock is overvalued by 66%. Investors today are paying a 66% premium to tangible book value—and for what, exactly?

The Numbers

IEP currently trades at a market cap of about $5B. On paper, its NAV stands at $3.7B. But dig deeper. Subtract goodwill ($288M) and intangibles ($395M), and tangible book value drops to roughly $3B. That means investors are paying 1.66x tangible NAV for a holding company—an asset structure that by definition should trade at or below NAV.

Most closed-end holding companies—from diversified private equity firms to Japanese trading houses—trade at discounts to NAV. Why? Because holding companies create double taxation and restrict an investor's ability to allocate capital themselves. In this particular case, paying a premium is even more baffling given IEP's deteriorating capital allocation, optimistic valuations, and an unsustainable dividend that will be discussed below.

Viskase

Envirodyne Industries (OTC:VKSC) holding of Viskase is the core of IEP's food packing sector. IEP has a controlling stake in the company, which in fact is publicly traded and valued at $115.8M as of 3/31/25 market close.

IEP's 91% stake would have a value of $105.4M. However, on their books they mark it as if the stake were to be worth $159M; that's a 50% markup over actual market value as of 3/31/25. They say that the company has a very low trading volume and assigned a 9x EBITDA multiple, which is also extremely high given how mature the food packing industry is and how much capital it requires to operate. Not to mention, they are excluding depreciation and amortization, which are key in the valuation of capital-intensive companies.

Dividend

IEP's 21% dividend yield is arguably the biggest reason retail chases the name. On any stock screener, it jumps out like a flashing green light. The highest dividend yield of any public company by far. However, there's a catch. This payout is only possible because Carl himself owns roughly 87% of IEP and has been taking most of his dividends in additional units rather than cash. This reduces the cash outlay needed to finance the payout for remaining unitholders but dilutes everyone else along the way.

Let's break this down. As of last quarter, IEP's business isn't making money—they're losing, and they've been doing so quarter after quarter. The company has about $2.2B in cash, but almost all of that is designated to pay off the large principal payments on its 2026 and 2027 senior notes. In other words, that cash is already spoken for. Once those debts are paid, IEP will be left with limited liquidity. They could potentially refinance, but I doubt they'll get anything below a 9% given where rates are right now and IEP's credit rating. Paying 9%/year in interest would not be ideal at all, as cash balance would decrease even faster.

Now consider what it actually costs to maintain the payout. With Icahn owning about 87% of the 546M outstanding units, the remaining 13% of public holders are still owed cash dividends. At $2.00 per unit annually, that's about $142M in cash/year just to fund these. But with the businesses losing money, that money would need to come from either selling off private businesses or liquidating public holdings.

The cracks are already showing. IEP slashed its dividend from $2.00 to $1.00 in August 2023 and again from $1.00 to $0.50 in November 2024—two straight 50% cuts in just 18 months. With no turnaround in sight and mounting debt obligations, the current structure is unsustainable, in my opinion.

The Icahn Game Isn't Working Anymore

Carl Icahn made his name forcing companies to unlock value. From Tappan and Texaco back in the 80s to Apple and eBay more recently, his reputation has always carried serious weight in boardrooms. But ironically, his own holding company now trades at a huge premium to its NAV, and there's really no clear catalyst to justify it.

If you look at his latest 13F, you'll notice big concentrated bets that just keep underperforming. Take JetBlue (JBLU), for example. Icahn nearly doubled his stake this quarter at prices between $4.72 and $8.31, but the stock's now sitting at $4.88, right near the low end of that buy range. He also increased his position in Illumina (ILMN) and trimmed down Southwest Gas (SWX), but neither move has created any meaningful buzz in the market.

Even his auto segment is struggling. Pep Boys (owned by Icahn Automotive Group LLC), which used to be a strong cash generator in his portfolio, has been underperforming for a while now. The automotive market is changing fast, and Pep Boys just hasn't kept up with the shift toward EV maintenance and more specialized service models.

There's no doubt Carl was a force of nature in his prime. But the market's different now, and his recent picks feel like relics of a past strategy that just doesn't work anymore: highly cyclical and underperforming across the board.

Risks

First, Carl Icahn himself. Despite his age, Icahn still has influence on Wall Street. If he were to engineer a major activist win or a lucrative sale of a private subsidiary at a premium, NAV could rise meaningfully, partially justifying the current premium to NAV. For example, if JetBlue miraculously turns around or if he manages to offload underperforming units like Pep Boys at attractive prices, that would boost tangible book value.

Also, there's the dividend. Even though I argue it's unsustainable above, markets can remain irrational. As long as Carl keeps taking his dividends in units instead of cash, it prolongs the life of this payout, which could support the stock price longer than fundamentals would suggest.

My Take

At the end of the day, IEP is trading at a massive 66% premium to tangible book value. We're talking about a holding company structure here, just a bunch of operating businesses and public stock holdings, some of which are valued at optimistic levels on its own books.

Their food packaging segment, which consists of Viskase (OTCPK:VKSC), owned by Envirodyne Industries, is marked at $159M on IEP's books. As of 3/31/25, their 91% stake was worth just $105.4M based on market prices. Today, with VKSC's market cap at $183M, that same stake would be worth about $167M. We'll have to see next quarter whether IEP continues valuing VKSC based on an EBITDA multiple or changes to market cap. The auto segment is in secular decline. The dividend has been cut twice in 18 months and still only exists because Carl takes his payouts in units rather than cash. And Carl's recent plays: JBLU, ILMN, and SWX aren't exactly lighting the world on fire.

At almost 90, Carl Icahn is a legend whose prime strategies may no longer match today's market reality. Investors are paying a hefty premium for the brand name on the door rather than what's inside the building. Personally, I wouldn't pay more than tangible book value here, which is roughly $3B market cap or 40% below today's price. Until IEP can generate real operating wins or fix its capital allocation problem, I just don't see the justification for this valuation.

data.title: Travel + Leisure: Improved Sales And Loan Quality Provide Further Upside
data.description:
Shares of Travel + Leisure (NYSE:TNL) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 40%. Investors received more good news this week as the company reported solid Q2 results, pushing shares to a new 52-week high. While Q2 EPS was mixed, the underlying composition of earnings was solid, and I was particularly impressed by better credit quality on its loan portfolio, which should help support earnings going forward.

Q2 Business Mix Outweighs A Modest EPS Miss

In the company’s second quarter, Travel + Leisure earned $1.65, a penny below expectations even as revenue grew by 4% from last year to $1.02 billion. The company generated $250 million of adjusted EBITDA, up 2% from last year as margins compressed by 20bps to 24.6%. TNL operates timeshare resorts, which it calls vacation ownership interests (“VOI”). Operating across multiple brands, it has about 270 resorts and 809k timeshare owners. While this is the primary focus, it also has an exchange network with over 3.3 million members where they can trade timeshares and book vacations.

Vacation ownership revenue grew by 6% to $853 million, and this unit is the core focus of the company. Conversely, travel and membership revenue fell by 6% to $166 million, given an 11% decline in exchange transactions. This was a bit weaker than expected, which drove the miss in EPS. Vacation clubs have been getting bigger via consolidation, and this trend has been a structural headwind for exchange activity. With TNL dedicating incremental capital to expanding its VOI business and essentially managing the decline in the exchange business, markets largely looked through the weakness in exchange activity.

VOI Sales Activity Is Encouraging

The vacation ownership business’s performance was particularly strong, and it appears that consumer engagement is improving, even with elevated economic uncertainty, aided by the fact that its average customer has a ~$120k household income, helping to insulate it a bit from weakness among lower-income households after years of excess inflation sapped them of buying power.

In Q2, net vacation sales were $474 million, up about 7% from last year. Gross sales were up 9%, but the company provisioned $128 million in provisions for losses, up 13% from last year. This provision gets netted against gross sales. The company is targeting a full-year provision of 21%, above its high-teens norm, to safeguard its loan book. As I will discuss below, its loan quality is improving, and this reserve stance may prove to be conservative, which I view favorably as it could support reserve releases in the future. Now, TNL is spending more to generate these sales, with marketing spending up ~6%, but this is consistent with its sales growth.

We are seeing increased engagement with its guests, and revenue per guest rose by 7% to $3,251. Tours were up 3%, but sales were up an excellent 9%, meaning consumers who take a tour are increasingly likely to buy a membership, which will result in higher future servicing and interest income. Indeed, property management fees rose by 3% to $217 million. Offsetting this, operating expenses rose by about 3.5% to $457 million.

These sales are a flywheel that help support ongoing revenue via interest payments, spending at resorts, and annual maintenance fees. Indeed, about 75% of the company’s revenue is recurring in nature, making its business model fairly resilient during an economic downturn, especially as 80% of its owners have already paid off their loan and own the VOI in full.

Loan Quality Is A Clear Positive

The primary risk to this model is a significant degradation in credit quality that leads to defaults on VOI loans. In Q2, consumer financing revenue grew by 1% to $112 million, and net of interest costs, it was flat at $78 million. It has $3.2 billion of gross receivables, about two-thirds of which sit inside of securitizations. TNL has $2 billion of non-recourse debt supporting its receivables. The payments on these loans go to pay off the securitizations, with TNL earning a net interest spread. In the event of a shortfall, TNL is not legally obligated to repay the debt.

Still, TNL wants the receivables inside the securitizations to perform well, as the better they perform, the more net interest income it retains. Importantly, over multiple years, TNL has been tightening its lending standards, which left the company well-positioned for some recent softness in consumer credit. Most notably, it now has a minimum FICO score of 640 on new originations, which should help reduce loan losses. As a result, its vacation rental receivables have about a ~750 FICO score and a 15% average interest rate.

You can see how recent vintages are stronger than older ones, with significantly less sub-600 FICO scores as a share of total loans. As older loans continue to roll off, this should support improved credit quality and fewer losses.

Indeed, we're seeing this materialize in results. The company carries $604 million in reserves, or about 19% of receivables. Reserves are down $10 million year to date as it has provisioned $219 million for loan losses and had $229 million of charge-offs. The company now has $149 million in 30+ day delinquencies, down from $171 million at the start of the year. With about 4x coverage of delinquencies, TNL’s reserves are strong, and after this year, I expect its provisioning rate to fall back below 20%, which will support increased 2026 earnings.

TNL has a solid balance sheet with $3.6 billion of recourse debt and $212 million in cash. It has a $350 million maturity in October at 6.6%, which it will roll over. TNL currently has 3.4x leverage. This will likely rise as it pre-funds its October maturity and then come back down as that maturity is paid off in Q4. The company has generated $123 million in free cash flow so far this year.

Capital Returns Support Further Upside

Given its solid balance sheet, TNL can focus cash flow on capital returns. Indeed, it did $70 million of Q2 repurchases. As a result, its share count is down by 6.3% from last year. On top of this, it pays a 3.6% dividend yield, which I view as secure. I would expect buybacks to support ~4% share count reduction over the next year.

Alongside results, TNL reiterated guidance for adjusted EBITDA of $955-$985 million this year. It expects gross sales of $2.4-$2.5 billion with volume per guest up to $3,250 from $3,050-$3,150 previously. I see some upside risk to gross sales given the higher VPG. While its 21% reserve rate may prove too conservative, I don't expect TNL to make a change this year, and I view lower reserves as primarily a 2026 upside risk.

Overall, TNL’s core business is performing well, with its ancillary exchange business facing ongoing pressure. Importantly, its loan book is well-positioned, and TNL has secure and predictable recurring cash flows underpinning its business. I see the business having about $250-$275 million of run-rate free cash flow capacity this year and closer to $300 million in 2026 as provisions improve.

That is about $4.20 per share. Even after their rally, TNL has a ~7% free cash flow yield, which supports meaningful capital returns. Given my view that TNL’s VOI receivables are particularly well-positioned and with rising sales engage, I view this as an attractive free cash flow yield. With free cash flow rising to $300 million next year, I see shares moving towards $70 in the next twelve months, providing about a 15% total return opportunity. As such, I view TNL as a “Buy."

data.title: GigaCloud Q2 Preview: Even With The Recent Recovery, It Is Still Very Cheap
data.description:
Introduction

GigaCloud Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:GCT) is expected to report its Q2 earnings on August 6, after the market closes, so I wanted to check what we should expect to see and what I am looking for in terms of outlook and the company’s internal performance improvements.

The last time I covered the company was almost a year ago, and since then, the company has seen a massive swing in investor sentiment for the worse, but it all seems to be becoming much more positive as the company’s share price has recovered handsomely since the April lows. My view on the company hasn’t changed. I’ve been adding at the lows and now will be holding through for the long haul if the company continues to head this direction. I will add more if the market once again overreacts.

What to Expect

The company expects to make $0.58 and $0.42 in non-GAAP and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $290m in sales. This implies 6.5% growth in sales sequentially and a decline of 6.5% y/y. In the last 90 days, there have been two downward revisions to the company’s EPS estimates.

How do these estimates stack up with what analysts expect? Analysts are looking for the company to make around $310m in sales, meaning the management is a lot more cautious about the company’s performance, which could lead to a miss in the headline numbers. However, in the last ten quarters, the company beat sales estimates 100% of the time while missing and meeting once on EPS estimates. I expect a decent beat in the upcoming quarter, as I believe management may be lowballing the company’s potential here. It appears that way every quarter.

Overall, it looks like the company is headed in the right direction. Sales are still down y/y, but the sequential growth in sales is promising, and I believe GCT will turn itself around over the next few quarters, depending on the macro environment.

What I’m Looking For

The company has experienced some decent declines in profitability in recent quarters due to tariff-driven supply chain problems, as well as higher ocean freight costs. This performance did not help the company’s sentiment to improve, leading to quite a decent decline in share price in April, when tariff threats were at their highest. I’m going to be looking for any comments regarding the company’s profitability going forward, and whether management sees a limited impact from the proposed tariffs, and how they will try to mitigate as much as possible.

The US is not the only market where the company can thrive, and that has been shown already by investments in international markets like the EU. As US sales began to decline, the fulfillment center in Germany almost doubled its sales figure in Q1’25, which helps the company diversify its dependence on the US market. I will be looking for more information regarding outside operations and how they will impact the company’s overall performance.

The company’s GMV figure last quarter came in much higher than a year before. A solid 56% growth y/y, due to the success of the European operations, which saw its GMV grow by around 80% y/y, driving active buyers close to 10,000 vs 5,500 last year.

I expect the momentum to be sustained in terms of GMV growth as well as active buyers, especially outside of the US, in regions like Europe and many others.

Why should we expect this momentum to continue? The company’s unique supplier fulfilled retailing (SFR) model now enables more than 50% of GMV from third-party sellers, which in turn increases the ecosystem’s longevity and resilience, so with an increase in active buyers of over 81%, and 33% increase in third-party sellers in the previous quarter, we can see that there is indeed acceleration in participation as well as there is plenty of stickiness of transactions. Further diversification efforts of its supply chain will be beneficial in the long run, especially if that means the company will manage to avoid the brunt of the tariffs.

For the US operations, I expect further pain ahead for a while, but in the long run, with the full integration of the Noble House, we will see profitability improving once again. In the previous quarter, the company retired over 400 SKUs, while introducing over 300 new SKUs. Noble House typically saw seasonality improvements in the summer quarter, so with streamlining of operations here, I expect to see improved inventory turnover and expand margins over time. I will be looking out for progress updates on this front, too. Typically, when a company replaces its SKUs with newer ones, the ramp-up in sales volumes takes around three to six months, so I am expecting further improvements overall over the next quarter or two.

Valuation

It has been a long time since I did a valuation model for GCT, so let’s dig right in.

For revenues, there aren’t many analysts covering the company, and the ones that do don’t show much improvement in sales growth. Therefore, I will be sticking to quite low revenue growth estimates, for conservatism’s sake. The company’s past growth performance, for example, the 3-year CAGR was around 54%, so a CAGR of 6% is rather conservative in my opinion.

For margins, I’m confident that in the long run, the company will improve its profitability even further. Also, I don’t think the company’s profitability is particularly bad at these levels either. Based on these assumptions, the company will be trading below double-digits in P/E.

For my DCF analysis, I went with the company’s rather high WACC of 13.6%, much higher than what I usually use, which is around 10%. Nevertheless, this will provide me with a decent margin of safety in the long run and stress test the company’s performance. Additionally, I went with a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

So, with these conservative (in my opinion) estimates, the company’s intrinsic value is around $34 a share, which means, even with the recent runup in share price, the company is still trading at a massive discount to its fair value.

Closing Comments

I’ve held the company in my portfolio through the big drop to below $12 a share, adding at around those levels, as well as $14, bringing my average much lower now and comfortably sitting in the green. I believe the company will continue to perform well in the future, and with further share buybacks, I could see the company rewarding its shareholders handsomely in the future.

During the recent turmoil, the company became one of my biggest holdings in the portfolio, and I remain confident in its ability to outperform in the long run; therefore, I am maintaining a buy rating.

If the company’s shares drop below $20 again on non-fundamentals but rather overreaction, I will not hesitate to add even more to my already decent position.

data.title: We Still Like Atlantic Union Bankshares
data.description:
Today we check back in and cover the just-reported earnings from Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (NYSE:AUB). We like the growing dividend and the yield here is still north of 4%. Thus far here in Q2, our coverage of regional banks which we consider 'pulse-checks' on local economies, indicates that said economies have been resilient. There have been little to no signs of fiscal stress despite risks from tariffs and somewhat softening economic data. In the universe of banks we cover, we have seen in select cases, loans and deposits decline, or efficiency soften. This all depends on the bank. Overall, they have held up very well. Even with the rebound in AUB stock with the market off April lows, we still think the bank stock makes a great long-term investment, on the back of a growing dividend and solid performance on the key metrics. Let us discuss.

Atlantic Union Bankshares' headline results and net interest income

Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation's revenue was $373 million, which was a 79% increase in this critical metric year-over-year, and beat estimates by $4.7 million. Of course, this huge surge stemmed from past acquisitions, and most recently, the pickup of Sandy Spring, completed earlier this year. Thus we expected big inorganic growth to be reported on those metrics. What about margins? We saw strength here as well. Net interest margin rose from the sequential Q1 by 40 basis points to 3.78%, which is incredibly strong. We saw a reduction in the cost of funds (dipping 1 basis point), but the yields on variable loans surged by 37 basis points. The margins on a tax equivalent basis thus were up 38 basis points sequentially to a solid 3.83%, ranking high among our universe of coverage. Net interest income was $321.4 million vs. $184.2 million in the sequential Q1, of course rising due to Sandy Spring.

So we mentioned that loans saw higher yields. The 38 basis point surge took yields to 6.05%. We think the bank could see margins over 4% if the Fed does not cut rates in Q3. We should also note that the provision for credit losses was $105 million, mostly reflecting the new assets held and a reserve built out. We monitor this metric from this current level as provisions do offset earnings power. EPS (adjusted) was $0.95, reflecting the larger size of the bank.

Atlantic Union Bankshares' loans, deposits, and asset quality

With a new set of assets under the belt of the company, we have a new baseline to monitor trends from. At the end of Q2, total loans were $27.3 billion and total deposits were $30.9 billion. These are the levels at which we will monitor performance going forward. Of course, we also have a new baseline for asset quality that considers all the new assets.

The allowance for credit losses was up. These allowances were up to $342 million, which was up from $209 million in Q1. A more meaningful metric is the allowance for credit losses as a percentage of all loans: this was 1.25% compared to 1.13% in Q1. This is a bump, which suggests additional allowances are being made as the bank looks to clean up loans on the books following the merger. We suspect the allowance improves in a few quarters. I

We can also still look at the return metrics that dipped from Q1. Here we look at adjusted numbers. The adjusted return on average assets improved to 1.46% from 0.90%, while the adjusted return on average equity jumped to 11.23% from 6.95%. Somewhat to our surprise, despite the higher return metrics, the efficiency ratio softened to 68.68% from 61.81% in Q1. This is on a tax equivalent basis. However, Q1 was the strongest mark for efficiency in many quarters, and a high 60% level tracks more closely with the historical average.

Dumping a bunch of loans

We also learned in the earnings release that the company sold off a chunk of commercial real estate loans. These sales were approximately $2.0 billion of said loans picked up in the Sandy Spring acquisition, which the bank marked to fair value at $1.84 billion. The bank picked up $1.87 billion from this, which increased the cash balance at the end of the quarter. Wisely, a good portion of the proceeds was used to repay a short-term Federal Home Loan Bank advance, as well as cover some brokered CDs that matured during the quarter.

Final thoughts

We like the bank for its dividend growth. During the quarter the bank also declared and paid cash dividends of $0.34 per share, 6.3% higher than a year ago. While shares are up from the springtime lows, we still like buying for an investment. The bank is larger now and more diversified. With over a 4% yield and a still positive outlook for the sector, we think there is continued upside here. Consider taking advantage of any weakness and buy for income and future capital appreciation.

data.title: DORK Are The New Meme Stocks, But There's A Lesson In The Madness
data.description:
Introduction

Just when it felt like the meme stock era had faded away, it came back completely uninvited. And this time, there’s a new group in the spotlight: the so-called DORK stocks. DORK is an acronym that stands for Krispy Kreme, Inc. (DNUT), Opendoor Technologies Inc. (OPEN), Rocket Companies, Inc. (RKT), and Kohl's Corporation (KSS). What do they have in common? The answer is nothing, really. Except for high short interest, soaring trading volumes, and a crowd of retail investors who suddenly decided to bring back the kind of hype we haven’t seen since the GameStop Corp. (GME) days. And the driver of this hype is different posts and signals on social media.

But the setup isn’t identical to what we saw in 2021. This time, there’s no clear enemy, no Robinhood-style crusade against Wall Street. What we’re seeing now is more subtle, but also more speculative. These are low-float, high-risk names that were mostly forgotten until Reddit (RDDT) posts flipped the switch. Why was it so simple to ignite the crowd, and should we join the rally? This is what I’ll try to answer today.

The Market Setup

This current market setup I want to call “When Confidence Meets Boredom.” Right now, the market is near an all-time high. That sounds great, but here’s the thing: volume hasn’t followed to the extent we would expect it to. The last three waves of the market’s breakouts were on the booming volumes. This time it’s different.

And that’s actually very important. When a market is at all-time highs with weaker volume, it tells you something very specific: most investors aren’t scared, and it’s easier to move the stocks. In general, have you really thought about what it means for a market to be at an all-time high? It means that most investors are either in the green or at the break-even point. Everyone is happy. Those who are still sitting on the sidelines with cash are most likely waiting for an excuse to get in. And lately, that excuse has been pure FOMO (fear of missing out).

Think about the psychology: a few months ago, everyone was still nervous about tariffs, inflation, and rates. But now, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has collapsed by more than 15% from earlier this year, and is trading near one of its lowest levels in the past 12 months. Investors are almost not hedging at moments like this, and when that kind of sentiment takes over, riskier bets start looking a lot more attractive. Especially the kind that can move 20%, 50%, or even 100% in a day.

I am not saying, by the way, that it’s right. And I am by no means suggesting you act on this article in any way. I am myself is sitting on a decent pile of cash I have been gathering for the past months and not getting in for now, because the whole setup “feels” weird for me. I often rely on the feeling I get when I buy something, and lately, that gut has been telling me to stay aside. Again, not suggesting you stay on the sidelines and miss the rally, just sharing my own thoughts.

DNUT - Krispy Kreme

Let’s talk about each one of the DORK selection, and I will start with the first one, DNUT. Six months ago, DNUT was down more than 50%, with the dead volume. The stock barely moved for weeks. And then, boom. It went from a forgotten consumer name to one of the most heavily traded tickers in the market.

To give you some numbers: DNUT’s average 3-month trading volume was around 7.7 million shares. Over the past 10 days, that jumped to more than 21 million shares. But two days ago, it wasn’t 21 million, it was almost 150 million shares in one day.

And then there’s the structure. DNUT has about 171 million shares outstanding, but only around 73 million are in the float. That’s not a lot, especially when more than 26% of that float is being shorted. Now add the fact that insiders hold over 54% of the company. That leaves very little room for trading, so when demand suddenly spikes, the price moves hard. And that’s exactly what happened.

OPEN - Opendoor Technologies

The second stock in line from the DORK acronym is OPEN. Even though I already covered it in depth a couple of days ago, it’s impossible to talk about this moment without mentioning it. OPEN is the stock that kicked this entire cycle off. In a single month, it was up nearly 800%. Then it pulled back, then it bounced again. At its peak, the stock was trading on about 1.8 billion shares in one day.

The company had high short interest (around 18.6%), thin float relative to its outstanding shares, and a “100-bagger” post from a hedge fund manager that went viral. The stock went violent. Once OPEN exploded, the market started looking for similar setups. And that’s where DORK came from. DNUT, RKT, and KSS were already floating in the background, mentioned occasionally, but not moving. OPEN’s rally dragged them with it, same as a few years ago, when GME was dragging AMC Entertainment Holdings (AMC) behind it.

RKT - Rocket Companies

Rocket Companies is a name that’s always had a strange relationship with retail traders. It’s not a meme stock in the traditional sense, but it shows up on Reddit again and again. At least I hear people mentioning it really often.

One Redditor posted a $130K YOLO trade on RKT with the caption, “Tastes like 2021 again.” That post alone pushed the stock up 15% in premarket trading. And that’s all it took to get the rest of the crowd involved. The stock has 138 million shares outstanding, but the float is just 116 million. Almost 50% of that float is sold short. Volume, which usually hovers around 15 million shares, jumped to nearly 25 million in the span of a few days. The price action wasn’t just random, it was triggered by the perfect combination of low float, heavy short interest, and an emotionally charged retail community looking for something to run. With this short percent of float and pretty heavy institutional holdings, I am surprised to see it’s not going parabolic right now.

KSS - Kohl's Corporation

Kohl’s deserves a section of its own, because what’s happening there is on another level. The stock surged more than 40% in just five days, with trading volume reaching over 200 million shares in a single day. And we’re talking about a $1.4 billion market cap company, and not some microcap.

But the real story is the short interest. 47.3% of outstanding shares are currently shorted. That’s nearly half the stock. And with institutions holding more than 100% of the float (yes, more than 100%), it becomes extremely hard for short sellers to exit their positions when the price starts moving against them. The result classic squeeze setup. It might look strange that more than 100% of Kohl’s shares are held by institutions, but it actually happens because of short selling. When institutions lend out their shares to short sellers, those shares get sold and bought again, so both the lender and the new buyer show up as owners. It creates double-counting, which is why you can see ownership reported at 107%. Can you imagine with this setup where the stock can fly if the social media community unites against it? Exciting and worrying at the same time.

With a float of about 111 million shares and insider ownership around 3%, there just isn’t enough liquidity when everyone wants out. KSS, like all of them, is not getting attention because of its business. It’s flying because it’s one of the most heavily shorted liquid stocks in the market.

This Is Not the Same as 2021

What makes this wave different is that it doesn’t have a mission. Back in 2021, the meme stock movement was emotional. It was about fighting back against hedge funds and winning one for the little guy. This time it’s just about making money and risking it. And while that might sound fine, I think it’s also more dangerous than back in 2021. This time around, there’s no coordination, no leadership, no plan, no goal. You can’t follow someone on Reddit and be comforted by getting updated in real time. Here, everyone is against the rest of the community, not against institutions. These rallies start fast, and they end just as fast, often after or before the market. You might not even have the chance to get out.

That said, the psychology is familiar. The market feels expensive. The big names have already run. So investors go hunting for the next big thing. When a stock with 25% short interest jumps 15% in one session, it grabs a lot of attention, and that attention fuels momentum.

Conclusion: Watch the DORKs

There is little sense in discussing the fundamentals of DNUT, OPEN, RKT, and KSS. Although when I broke down OPEN, I was surprised to learn it’s not a completely “empty” business. But that’s irrelevant here. These names are moving because the market decided they were worth speculating on. And in a lower-volume, FOMO environment, that’s often all it takes.

But, I wouldn’t be ignoring it either. If you’re watching the market and ignoring what’s happening with meme stocks, you’re missing a huge part of what’s driving sentiment right now. We can learn from it about the moods of retail and act on it in other ways. Someone might decide to jump right in, to try his or her luck, and it’s okay, just remember to go into this with the mindset of most likely losing all.

Take it as a casino - a fun game that’s very hard to win. The odds are against you. If you are not feeling opportunistic, you can make your assumptions based on the information provided and extrapolate them to other securities. This article offers food for thought, as some of the readers wrote not long ago. Just remember what always comes next: when the music stops, someone’s left holding the bag, or left without the chair, however this game is played in your country. If you’re going to trade these names, know what you’re doing, know your timing, and don’t mistake momentum for safety.

data.title: A Safe Place To Land: How And Where To Park Cash (Especially In Retirement)
data.description:
In volatile markets, uncertainty often breeds hesitation. Over the past several months, we’ve received more questions about where to park cash than ever before - especially from retirees or those nearing retirement. You’re not alone if you’re feeling cautious or unsure. When everything feels expensive or unstable, keeping money in cash can seem like the only safe choice.

But let’s unpack this a little.

Is parking your cash the right long-term decision - or a temporary pause in a more important plan? Here’s how to approach it smartly, and where to put your money while you wait.

Why You Might Want to Park Cash

Parking cash usually stems from two situations. Either you’re nervous about market conditions and want to wait for clarity, or you’re retired (or approaching retirement) and want cash on hand for income stability.

In both cases, you are likely seeking investments with similar characteristics. You want your money to be easy to access, so liquidity is essential. You also want to ensure that your capital is protected—safety is non-negotiable. Stability is important too, because you don’t want to risk your cash reserve fluctuating in value. Finally, you’d prefer if your parked cash generated some kind of return while you wait for better opportunities.

High-Interest Savings Accounts (HISAs), money market funds, and ultra-short bond ETFs generally meet these needs. But not all options are equal—and some come with hidden downsides.

What Most People Get Wrong About Parking Cash

Let’s start with an important truth: HISAs are commodities. Most of them offer the same basic benefits—safety, liquidity, and modest yield. Yet many investors obsess over finding the “best” rate.

Here’s the catch: the difference between a 3.89% and 4.16% yield on $100,000 is roughly $270 annually. After taxes, you’re looking at about $135. That’s hardly a game-changer for your financial future.

Despite this, many investors spend hours hunting for a few extra basis points in yield. That time would be better spent reviewing your investment plan or learning more about companies on your watchlist.

What often gets overlooked is that HISA returns are:

  • Not tax-efficient in non-registered accounts.
  • Often reduced once central banks start cutting interest rates.
  • Poor at protecting against inflation over time.

Worse, sitting on the sidelines waiting for the “right moment” to invest means exposing yourself to opportunity cost. Since January 2021, both U.S. and Canadian markets have climbed over 65%, despite endless headlines forecasting doom. If you stayed out of the market waiting for confirmation, you missed out.

A Better Reason to Park Cash: The Cash Wedge

If you’re retired, cash becomes more than a fear-based decision - it’s a strategic tool. That’s where the cash wedge concept comes in.

The idea is simple: during a market downturn, you use your cash reserve to fund your expenses instead of selling stocks at depressed prices. This buffer gives your portfolio time to recover, preserving your capital and dividend-paying stocks.

Here’s how to build your cash wedge:

  • Calculate how much income your portfolio generates annually (e.g., $1M at 3% = $30,000).
  • Determine your annual retirement budget (e.g., $50,000).
  • Find the shortfall ($20,000 in this case).
  • Multiply that shortfall by three years = $60,000 cash wedge.

That $60,000 can sit in a HISA or money market ETF. It’s not about chasing the highest yield; it’s about providing peace of mind and flexibility.

Risks of Parking Too Much Cash

While having a cash wedge in retirement makes sense, overdoing it can lead to unintended consequences.

Key risks include:

  • Erosion of purchasing power: Inflation silently reduces what your money can buy. For example, with a 4% HISA yield taxed at 40%, you net $2,400. But with 2.5% inflation, you’ll need $102,500 to maintain purchasing power. You actually lost $100.
  • Falling interest rates: Today’s yields may not last. As rates decline, your return will likely shrink.
  • No timing signals from the market: You’ll never get a perfect cue that it’s time to reinvest. Every year since 2021 has felt risky—and yet markets climbed.
  • Opportunity cost: Parking $100,000 in cash instead of investing it in the market could have cost you $50,000 in gains over the past few years.

If you’re parking cash because you’re unsure, ask yourself: What would give me the conviction to reinvest?

How to Pick a Good Cash Option

If you’ve decided that a cash reserve is appropriate, don’t overcomplicate your selection process. Focus on the fundamentals.

Look for options that are:

  • Liquid: You need to access funds quickly without penalties.
  • Safe and stable: Stick with well-regarded banks or large ETF providers.
  • Tax-efficient: In non-registered accounts, consider products like HSAV that reinvest income.
  • Low-fee: A lower Management Expense Ratio (MER) improves net returns.
  • Currency-aligned: Avoid USD options unless your spending is in U.S. dollars—currency swings can erase gains.

You can also use the DSR ETF stock screener or another ETF screener to find cash-equivalent ETFs. Simply search for keywords like “money” or “cash” and browse options in both CAD and USD.

Final Thoughts: Cash is a Tool, Not a Strategy

Cash has its place—but it shouldn’t be your plan. If you’re retired, a 3-year cash wedge makes sense. If you’re nervous, go back to your investment thesis and revisit why you invest in the first place.

Parking cash can be smart—but don’t let it become permanent.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
